title: The Point Where Thought Begins to See — Steiner on Goethe's Living Thinking
author: Rudolf Steiner / Goethe

body.text: Goethe discovered a third possibility between two dead ends. Thinking should not stand above nature and explain it from a distance. Nor could observation alone ever reveal the living world. He found a thinking that remains faithful to perception until perception itself begins to disclose its inner law.
Goethe's world-conception reached its highest state of maturity. Goethe knows no dead, spiritless matter.

Steiner recognised this as Goethe's greatest epistemological discovery. The observer does not impose ideas upon nature. Through disciplined attention, the formative idea already working within nature gradually becomes visible to consciousness. Imaginative perception begins when thought stops replacing the phenomenon and learns to accompany it.

body.caption: Thought accompanying nature — the observer learning to move with the phenomenon.

body.text: Polarity inheres in the phenomena of Nature. All that is sensible is also spiritual, all that is spiritual, sensible.

Polarity and intensification are the two principles. Polarity explains why nature reveals herself through opposites — expansion implies contraction, light implies darkness, leaf implies blossom. Intensification explains why development is revelation — the formative idea shines more clearly through the forms it creates.

The same organ which on the stem expanded as a leaf, and assumed the most varied forms, may now contract into the calyx, expand again in the petal, contract in the organs of reproduction, and finally expand for the last time as fruit.

Goethe's observer accompanies transformation. A cotyledon does not disappear when the leaf arrives. The leaf does not vanish when the petal unfolds. Each organ preserves the movement that preceded it while preparing what follows. The imagination becomes a faculty of continuity — perceiving what remains identical through continual change.

body.caption: Continuity through change — the imagination learning to perceive identity in transformation.

body.text: The individual parts as proceeding from the whole.

Steiner carries Goethe one step further. Goethe transformed perception. Steiner asks the observer to observe thinking itself. When thinking becomes its own object, consciousness discovers that genuine concepts arise through participation in the same creative activity that expresses itself throughout nature. The observer discovers that imagination is disciplined participation.

Remain with the phenomenon. Allow thought to become as living as the thing observed. Permit imagination to arise only where faithful perception has prepared it. Eventually the distinction between seeing and understanding softens. The phenomenon ceases to merely appear. It begins to teach.
